feature_name: PCIe Root Complex mode and LTSSM control
description: Supports operation as Root Complex with a defined initialization sequence. Software disables LTSSM before configuration then re-enables it to start link training. Link completion is verified by reading LTSSM_STATE equal to 0x11 in the DEBUG0 register. Sequence includes programming device mode and required configuration registers. Enumeration proceeds only after successful training.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.11.1.1 Initialization Sequence; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]; DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]
dependencies: PSC enable PCIESSEN; DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE set to RC; SerDes PLL locked; base at 0xA1008000
Remarks: Follow the documented step order strictly and poll state transitions to avoid race conditions.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock monitoring
description: SerDes PLL must be programmed and enabled for PCIe operation. The reference clock is provided to the SerDes and PLL lock must be confirmed. Software samples the LOCK bit in the SerDes Status register. Operation should not proceed until lock is asserted. Incorrect PLL setup prevents link training.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2.3 SerDes Configuration; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL; PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]
dependencies: Valid reference clock; SerDes PLL configuration; clocking domain enabled
Remarks: Use device recommended PLL settings and include timeouts for LOCK polling.

feature_name: Module power and clock domain enable via DEVSTAT
description: The PCIe module requires its power and clock domains to be enabled. Device level register DEVSTAT has bits to enable the PCIe module and select mode. Enabling must precede any register access or configuration. Power and Sleep Controller guidance applies per device manual. Failure to enable domains results in bus errors.
dependencies: PSC configuration; DEVSTAT write access; SoC clock tree ready
Remarks: Gate and ungate clocks only when link is idle and drivers are quiesced.

description: The controller supports 32 MSI vectors grouped with shared event numbers. Enable and disable are managed via MSIn_IRQ_EN_SET and MSIn_IRQ_EN_CLR registers. Status is reported in MSIn_IRQ_STATUS and cleared by write 1 to clear semantics. End of interrupt requires writing IRQ_EOI with the event number. Driver must handle vector to CPU event mapping.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Section 2-39 MSI overview; Table 2-10 PCIESS Interrupt Events; MSIn_IRQ_STATUS RAW/STATUS registers (e.g., 3-27 to 3-29)
dependencies: Configure MSIn_IRQ_EN_*; program IRQ_EOI; map events to CPU interrupt controller
Remarks: Always clear status then signal EOI to prevent lost or repeated interrupts.

feature_name: Legacy INTx interrupt support (mutually exclusive with MSI)
description: Endpoints can use either legacy INTx or MSI but not both simultaneously. Selection is managed during configuration and enumeration. Legacy signaling shares the race hazards noted for MSI. System software must choose one method per endpoint. Mixing modes leads to undefined behavior.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2-39: Each endpoint can use MSI or legacy interrupts but not both
Remarks: Prefer MSI for scalability and lower latency when supported by both ends.

feature_name: PCIe configuration space and capabilities layout
description: The controller exposes a 1 KB configuration space with standard and extended capabilities. Capabilities include Power Management, MSI, PCI Express, Extended Capabilities, and Port Logic. Capability structures are linked via next pointers starting from the header. Software walks the list to discover features. Proper parsing is essential during enumeration.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2-19 1 KB configuration space note; Table 2-7 Layout of PCIe Configuration Registers
dependencies: Config space access mechanism available; correct endianness handling
Remarks: Validate capability pointers to avoid loops and handle absent capabilities gracefully.

feature_name: Inbound address translation with four regions
description: Inbound transactions can be translated to internal bus addresses using up to four regions. Each region uses BAR match, PCIe start address high and low, and an internal address offset. The translated address is computed relative to the matched start. Regions allow mapping discontiguous PCIe ranges to local memory. Correct setup is required for inbound TLP acceptance.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2-13 IB_BARn; IB_STARTn_HI/LO; IB_OFFSETn; Tables 3-89 to 3-96 index
dependencies: Program IB_BARn, IB_STARTn_HI/LO, IB_OFFSETn; corresponding BAR configured
Remarks: Order programming so BARs and inbound regions are coherent before enabling link.

feature_name: BAR0 fixed mapping to application registers
description: BAR0 inbound space is reserved for application registers and cannot be remapped. Any inbound access matching BAR0 reaches the application registers block. This behavior applies in both RC and EP modes. It enables control and MSI operation even without endpoint software. There is no support to disable BAR0 register access.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2.7.2.2 BAR0 Exception for In-Bound Address Translation
dependencies: BAR0 must be present; application registers at 0xA1008000
Remarks: Do not attempt to repurpose BAR0 for memory windows; use other BARs for data access.

feature_name: BAR1 start address as implicit inbound base option
description: Inbound translation for BAR1 can use the BAR1 value as the start address when the region start is programmed as zero. In this mode only one inbound translation window is available and it is relative to BAR1. Other BARs or additional inbound windows cannot be used with this option. This simplifies configuration when using a single inbound window. Use with caution due to the limitation.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2.7.2.3 Using BAR1 Value As Start Address
dependencies: BAR1 configured; one inbound region enabled with start set to zero
Remarks: Document the mode in firmware to avoid conflicting region programming later.

feature_name: BAR types, masks, and configuration examples
description: The device supports standard BAR types including 64-bit non-prefetchable, 32-bit memory, and I/O BARs. BAR mask registers define region sizes and decode. Type 1 configuration is typical for RC and Type 0 for EP. Example configurations illustrate mixing BAR widths and attributes. Proper sizing ensures correct host allocation.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2.7.3 Use of BARs; Figure 2-4 Example BAR Configuration; BAR Mask Registers references
dependencies: Accurate size masks; alignment to power-of-two; endpoint or root mode selected
Remarks: Probe BAR sizes per PCIe spec by writing all 1s then restore and program masks.

description: Advanced Error Reporting registers are implemented to enhance diagnostics. End-to-End CRC generation and checking can be enabled via PCIE_ACCR. Bits expose capability and control for ECRC generation and checking. First error pointer and captured header words support failure analysis. Enable only when interoperability requires ECRC.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2.16.2.3.1 ECRC Generation and Checking; PCIE_ACCR bits (Table 3-177)
dependencies: Access to PCIE_ACCR; compatible peers; potential performance impact evaluated
Remarks: Log and clear AER status promptly to avoid masking subsequent errors.

feature_name: Link error handling and retraining controls
description: Link-related errors are handled at the Root Port with status and control fields. Software can force a link retrain by setting RETRAIN_LINK and monitors LINK_TRAINING to completion. Root Control registers allow mapping fatal, non-fatal, and correctable errors to system error signaling. PME Interrupt enable allows interrupt on PME messages. This centralizes recovery actions in the root.
evidence: SPRUGS6D 2.16.2.2.3 Link Errors; LINK_STAT_CTRL[RETRAIN_LINK, LINK_TRAINING]; ROOT_CTRL_CAP SERR bits; PME Interrupt Enable
dependencies: Root Complex role; access to Link Status/Control and Root Control registers
Remarks: Throttle retries and add backoff to prevent oscillation during unstable link conditions.

description: Power Management capability structure is present in configuration space. Fields include capability ID, next capability pointer, PME specification version, and related controls. PME clock is hardwired as noted. Software navigates via the next pointer to discover PM structure. Proper PM setup enables low power features negotiation.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Table 3-150 Power Management Capability fields; Table 2-7 capability offsets
dependencies: Capability pointer chain intact; system PM policy; host cooperation
Remarks: Validate PME support across link partners before enabling wake signaling.

description: The controller provides LTSSM state visibility via the DEBUG0 register. MSI Raw Status registers expose unmasked interrupt vector state and are primarily for debug. Raw status registers support write-1-to-set for testing. Using these aids during bring-up accelerates fault isolation. Production code should avoid relying on RAW writes.
evidence: SPRUGS6D DEBUG0 LTSSM_STATE (2.11.1.1); MSI*_IRQ_STATUS_RAW notes (3-27 to 3-29)
dependencies: Access to application registers; safe lab environment for raw testing
Remarks: Gate debug paths in release builds and prefer enabled-status views in runtime checks.

feature_name: Reset behavior and sticky register fields
description: A soft reset resets the PCIe subsystem while preserving sticky bits in memory-mapped registers. Documentation clarifies sticky field behavior in the Registers section. Drivers must reinitialize non-sticky configuration after reset. Sticky fields retain state across soft resets and should be cleared intentionally if required. Reset sequencing affects link and interrupt state.
evidence: SPRUGS6D change log references Page 2-34; Registers section sticky fields update
dependencies: Reset control access; reinitialization sequence; awareness of preserved bits
Remarks: Include a post-reset audit of sticky fields to avoid inheriting stale configuration.

feature_name: EDMA-based DMA transfer usage over PCIe (examples)
description: The documentation provides EDMA transfer examples involving PCIe addressing. Examples include pcieAddr definitions and buffer offset computation guidance. This indicates intended use with the device EDMA for efficient data movement. Software configures EDMA channels to source or sink PCIe mapped addresses. Proper cache and coherency handling is required in software.
evidence: SPRUGS6D change log: EDMA transfer examples and pcieAddr definition (Page 2-23)
dependencies: EDMA controller availability; BAR and translation set correctly; cache maintenance
Remarks: Use aligned buffers and program EDMA PaRAM with translated addresses to avoid TLP aborts.}]}
